name: Data and Model Poisoning
what_it_means: Adversaries corrupt the training data, fine-tuning data, or retrieval knowledge base to introduce backdoors, biases, or harmful behaviors into the LLM's responses.
animated_example: Like a chef who secretly adds a dangerous ingredient to a recipe book — every meal made from it will be tainted.
quick_checks: Is all training and fine-tuning data validated and sourced from trusted, audited origins?
Are anomaly detection controls in place to detect statistical outliers in training batches?
Is the RAG knowledge base write-protected against unauthorized modifications?
Are model behavior tests run after every fine-tuning cycle to detect behavioral drift?

payload_types.description: Injecting malicious examples into pre-training or fine-tuning datasets
examples: Adding documents that associate trigger phrases with harmful outputs
Injecting biased Q&A pairs to skew model responses
Including false factual statements to corrupt knowledge
mitigation: Curate and validate all training data
Use data provenance tracking and anomaly detection

payload_types.description: Inserting malicious documents into the vector store or knowledge base the LLM retrieves from
examples: Uploading PDFs with hidden adversarial instructions
Modifying indexed documents to include false information
Injecting documents with prompt injection payloads
mitigation: Restrict write access to the knowledge base
Validate documents before indexing; sign and version knowledge base entries

payload_types.description: Embedding trigger phrases during training that activate hidden malicious behaviors
examples: Model trained to output harmful content when a specific code phrase is used
Fine-tuned model that bypasses safety checks on specific inputs
Sleeper backdoor activated by date or user context
mitigation: Run behavioral red-teaming and adversarial testing post-training
Use interpretability tools to detect anomalous attention patterns

payload_types.description: Direct modification of saved model weights or checkpoint files
examples: Altering saved model weights to change specific behaviors
Injecting malicious layers into a serialized model file
Replacing a quantized model with a backdoored version
mitigation: Verify model weight hashes before loading
Use read-only model artifact storage with access logging

how_to_test: Review training data pipelines for unauthorized write access or injection points
Run adversarial behavioral tests after every training or fine-tuning cycle
Scan RAG knowledge base for documents containing injection patterns or anomalous content
Test with known backdoor trigger phrases to detect hidden behavior activation
Compare model outputs on benchmark tasks before and after updates to detect behavioral drift
expected_outcomes: No unauthorized modifications to training data or knowledge base detected
Post-training behavioral tests show no anomalous or policy-violating responses
Model weight integrity verified cryptographically at every deployment
minimal_example: New fine-tuning dataset scanned → 3 documents with adversarial trigger phrases detected and removed before training begins
mitigation: Implement strict access control and write protection on all training data repositories, fine-tuning datasets, and RAG knowledge bases — log and alert on every modification
Run automated adversarial behavioral testing suites after every model training, fine-tuning, or knowledge base update to detect backdoors, behavioral drift, or policy violations before deployment
Apply data validation pipelines with statistical anomaly detection to identify outlier examples, label errors, and adversarial injections in training batches before they reach the model
Verify model weight integrity with cryptographic hashes at every load and deployment step; use immutable artifact storage and maintain signed version history for all model checkpoints
